title: The day I stopped trying to fix him
summary: Describe the moment you realised you could not help a friend by trying to fix their life for them, and what changed about the friendship afterwards.
topic: When you stop trying to fix someone
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about the day you realised you had to stop trying to fix a friend's life for them, and what your friendship became afterwards. Use the past perfect, present perfect, a modal of past obligation ('should have'), and 'although' or 'however'.
hintStarters: I had been trying to fix Karim's life for almost four years, in the way you do when a close friend keeps making the same mistake in slightly different shapes, and the afternoon in a Cairo coffee shop when I finally stopped was…
If I am honest, I had treated him for a long time as a project rather than a person, which is something I should have noticed earlier because…
Although stepping back felt at first like a kind of betrayal, what I have come to understand is that what he needed from me was never advice but…

hintVocab.meaningVi: đưa lời khuyên không được yêu cầu
tôn trọng lựa chọn của ai đó
cảm thấy có trách nhiệm với

hintVocab.phrase: to accept someone as they are
hintVocab.meaningVi: chấp nhận ai đó như họ là
có mặt mà không phán xét

sampleText:
I had been trying to fix Karim's life for almost four years, in the way you do when a close friend keeps making the same mistake in slightly different shapes. He had quit his third job in two years, fallen out with a new partner the week before her birthday, and called me late one evening from a Cairo coffee shop to ask, again, what he should do. I had prepared a small speech on the bus over. I had practised the order of my points. When I sat down across from him and saw his face — tired, embarrassed, ready to argue — I realised that I had been giving him versions of this same speech since we were twenty-six, and that nothing in his life had changed in the direction I had been recommending. I closed my notebook and asked him what kind of coffee he wanted.

If I am honest, I had treated him for a long time as a project rather than a person, which is something I should have noticed earlier. My advice was always reasonable. I was usually right about what he was doing wrong. What I had not understood was that being right about someone's life is not the same as being useful to them. Every time I delivered another careful diagnosis, I confirmed for him that he was something to be solved, which was almost certainly the message that had been making his decisions worse all along. The afternoon in that coffee shop, I stopped. I did not announce that I was stopping. I just listened, asked one question, and said almost nothing else for an hour. He talked for a while, then went quiet, then said, in a small voice, that he had not realised how exhausting it must have been to be my friend during those years.

What has changed between us since that afternoon is harder to describe than I would have predicted. He has not solved his life. He still moves jobs, still has difficult relationships, still calls me late at night. However, the calls are different now. I am no longer waiting for the chance to deliver a plan; he is no longer waiting for the lecture and then resenting me for it. Although stepping back felt at first like a kind of betrayal — as though I were abandoning a friend who clearly needed help — what I have come to understand is that what he needed from me was never advice but the small, steady experience of being listened to by someone who had stopped trying to improve him. He is allowed, like the rest of us, to be a complicated and slowly-changing adult. My job, it turns out, was to let him be one.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Anh ấy đã bỏ công việc thứ ba trong hai năm

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I had prepared a small speech on the bus over
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Tôi đã chuẩn bị một bài nói nhỏ trên xe buýt đến đó

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I had practised the order of my points

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect and past perfect continuous for layered backstory
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I had been trying to fix Karim's life for almost four years', 'He had quit his third job', 'I had prepared a small speech', 'I had practised the order of my points' đều dùng quá khứ hoàn thành (và quá khứ hoàn thành tiếp diễn) để xếp lớp các sự kiện đã xảy ra trước thời điểm chính trong câu chuyện.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I had been trying to fix Karim's life for almost four years', 'He had quit his third job', 'I had prepared a small speech', 'I had practised the order of my points' all use the past perfect (and past perfect continuous) to layer events that happened before the main moment in the narrative.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal of past regret: 'should have + past participle'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Which is something I should have noticed earlier' dùng 'should have + quá khứ phân từ' để diễn đạt sự tiếc nuối về một điều đáng lẽ đã làm trong quá khứ nhưng không làm. Đây là một cấu trúc cực kỳ hữu ích trong văn phản tỉnh.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Which is something I should have noticed earlier' uses 'should have + past participle' to express regret about something that should have been done in the past but was not. This is a highly useful structure in reflective writing.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tôi đã cố gắng 'sửa' cuộc đời Karim gần bốn năm, theo cách bạn làm khi một người bạn thân cứ phạm cùng một lỗi trong những hình dạng hơi khác nhau. Anh ấy đã bỏ công việc thứ ba trong hai năm, cãi nhau với một người bạn gái mới một tuần trước sinh nhật của cô ấy, và gọi cho tôi vào một buổi tối muộn từ một quán cà phê ở Cairo để hỏi, lần nữa, anh nên làm gì. Tôi đã chuẩn bị một bài nói nhỏ trên xe buýt đến đó. Tôi đã luyện tập thứ tự các luận điểm của mình. Khi tôi ngồi xuống đối diện anh ấy và thấy gương mặt anh — mệt mỏi, xấu hổ, sẵn sàng cãi — tôi nhận ra rằng tôi đã đưa cho anh ấy các phiên bản của cùng bài nói này từ khi chúng tôi hai mươi sáu tuổi, và không có gì trong cuộc đời anh đã thay đổi theo hướng tôi đã khuyến nghị. Tôi đóng cuốn sổ và hỏi anh muốn cà phê loại gì.

Nếu thật lòng, tôi đã đối xử với anh ấy trong một thời gian dài như một dự án thay vì một con người, điều mà tôi đáng lẽ phải nhận ra sớm hơn. Lời khuyên của tôi luôn hợp lý. Tôi thường đúng về điều anh ấy đang làm sai. Điều tôi đã không hiểu là đúng về cuộc sống của ai đó không giống với hữu ích cho họ. Mỗi lần tôi đưa ra một chẩn đoán cẩn thận khác, tôi khẳng định cho anh ấy rằng anh là điều cần được giải quyết, thông điệp gần như chắc chắn đã làm các quyết định của anh ấy tệ hơn suốt thời gian qua. Buổi chiều trong quán cà phê đó, tôi dừng lại. Tôi không tuyên bố rằng tôi dừng lại. Tôi chỉ lắng nghe, hỏi một câu, và gần như không nói gì khác trong một giờ. Anh nói một lúc, rồi im lặng, rồi nói, bằng giọng nhỏ, rằng anh đã không nhận ra việc là bạn của tôi trong những năm đó hẳn đã mệt mỏi thế nào.

Điều đã thay đổi giữa chúng tôi kể từ buổi chiều đó khó mô tả hơn tôi đã dự đoán. Anh ấy không giải quyết được cuộc đời mình. Anh vẫn chuyển việc, vẫn có những mối quan hệ khó khăn, vẫn gọi tôi vào đêm khuya. Tuy nhiên, các cuộc gọi giờ khác. Tôi không còn chờ đợi cơ hội đưa ra một kế hoạch nữa; anh ấy không còn chờ đợi bài giảng rồi giận tôi vì điều đó nữa. Mặc dù lùi lại lúc đầu cảm thấy như một loại phản bội — như thể tôi đang bỏ rơi một người bạn rõ ràng cần giúp đỡ — điều tôi đã dần hiểu là điều anh ấy cần từ tôi chưa bao giờ là lời khuyên mà là trải nghiệm nhỏ và đều đặn của việc được lắng nghe bởi ai đó đã ngừng cố cải thiện anh ấy. Anh ấy được phép, như tất cả chúng ta, là một người lớn phức tạp và thay đổi chậm. Hóa ra công việc của tôi là để anh ấy được như vậy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses past perfect continuous correctly?
options: I have been trying to fix Karim's life for almost four years.
I had been try to fix Karim's life for almost four years.
I had been trying to fix Karim's life for almost four years.
I had being trying to fix Karim's life for almost four years.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect continuous = 'had been + -ing'. The context (looking back from a past moment) requires past perfect, not present perfect.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'should have + past participle' correctly for past regret?
options: Which is something I should noticed earlier.
Which is something I should have notice earlier.
Which is something I should had noticed earlier.
Which is something I should have noticed earlier.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Should have + past participle' expresses past regret. 'Have' stays as 'have' after the modal; the participle is 'noticed'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Should have + past participle' for past regret.

options: Although stepping back felt at first like a kind of betrayal, what he needed from me was never advice.
Although stepping back felt at first like a kind of betrayal, but what he needed from me was never advice.
Although, stepping back felt at first like a kind of betrayal what he needed from me was never advice.
Stepping back felt at first like a kind of betrayal although however what he needed from me was never advice.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses past perfect correctly?
options: Nothing in his life have changed in the direction I had been recommending.
Nothing in his life had changed in the direction I had been recommending.
Nothing in his life had change in the direction I had been recommending.
Nothing in his life had been change in the direction I had been recommending.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle' ('changed'); past perfect continuous in the relative clause 'I had been recommending'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'as though' + past subjunctive correctly?
options: Stepping back felt at first like a kind of betrayal — as though I am abandoning a friend.
Stepping back felt at first like a kind of betrayal — as though I would abandon a friend.
Stepping back felt at first like a kind of betrayal — as though I were abandoning a friend.
Stepping back felt at first like a kind of betrayal — as though I being abandoning a friend.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'as though' for an unreal current comparison, use the past subjunctive 'were' (for all persons).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses present perfect for an ongoing change correctly?
options: What have changed between us since that afternoon is harder to describe than I would have predicted.
What has change between us since that afternoon is harder to describe than I would have predicted.
What is changed between us since that afternoon is harder to describe than I would have predicted.
What has changed between us since that afternoon is harder to describe than I would have predicted.
mcQuestions.explanation: Subject 'what' is treated as singular → 'has changed'; past participle is 'changed'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'no longer + verb' correctly?
options: I am no longer waiting for the chance to deliver a plan.
I am no longer wait for the chance to deliver a plan.
I no longer am waiting for the chance to deliver a plan.
I do not no longer waiting for the chance to deliver a plan.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'No longer' goes after 'be' (or auxiliary): 'I am no longer waiting'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'treat X as Y' correctly?
options: I had treated him for a long time like a project rather than a person.
I had treated him for a long time as a project rather than a person.
I had treated him for a long time for a project rather than a person.
I had treated him for a long time to a project rather than a person.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Treat X as Y' is the standard collocation. 'Treat X like Y' is also possible but more colloquial; the others are wrong.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'must have + past participle' inside reported speech?
options: He had not realised how exhausting it must has been to be my friend.
He had not realised how exhausting it must been to be my friend.
He had not realised how exhausting it must have been to be my friend.
He had not realised how exhausting it must of been to be my friend.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Must have + past participle' is the modal of past deduction. 'Must of' is a common spelling error.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'allowed to + base verb' correctly?
options: He is allowed, like the rest of us, to being a complicated and slowly-changing adult.
He is allowed, like the rest of us, for be a complicated and slowly-changing adult.
He is allow, like the rest of us, to be a complicated and slowly-changing adult.
He is allowed, like the rest of us, to be a complicated and slowly-changing adult.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Be allowed to + base verb' is the standard structure; 'allowed' is the past participle used as the passive adjective.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'turn out' correctly to mean 'happen to be the case'?
options: My job, it turns out, was to let him be one.
My job, it turn out, was to let him be one.
My job, it turns over, was to let him be one.
My job, it turns to, was to let him be one.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It turns out (that)' is the standard parenthetical for revealing something previously unclear; subject 'it' takes 'turns'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If 'fixing' a friend almost always backfires, where is the line between unhelpful interference and real, useful support? Are there situations in which insisting on advice — even unwelcome advice — is the right thing to do?
